speaker: interviewer
text: 9th of October 1995, Irene Hirschfeld, tape three. You were saying that you were-- this train, this transport was sent to Auschwitz. Can you describe to me what happened once you got there? Did you feel that you'd been betrayed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not only us, but the German women that were watching us were surprised that they had to get off the train also. They thought we were all going straight to Brinnlitz. But we stayed there three weeks. And one evening somebody came and asked who knows how to knit? So I said, I know how to knit. They gave me wires, probably parts of the electrical wires that went around the camp and some yarn. And I was knitting gloves. And because I was doing it, I didn't have to get up at 4 o'clock in the morning and go to be counted outside. So you never know what--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were the only one?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I was the only way because I was the only one that was knitting.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened? When you got to the camp, what did they do with you-- with your transport?

speaker: interviewee
text: They pushed us to Birkenau and that's it. You stay here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to walk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course we had to walk. It wasn't a very long walk. But we walked from the train to the camp, itself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any-- any German officers, like Mengele, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mengele, I saw the-- the next day, of course. Sure. There was a selection, the first selection. Mengele came out. And whoever he decided should go, an elderly woman, was no question.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of Schindler's people--

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of Schindler's people too, yes. This was the-- the group of Schindler's people that Mengele came to greet.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what? There was nobody there to protect you people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. At that time, no. And we stayed three weeks. And we didn't believe anymore that we'll go ever to Schindler, but after three weeks, they told us to get ready. And put us on train. And we traveled for I think two weeks. I don't know. Maybe even longer from Auschwitz to Brinnlitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were saying something about after three days something happened. You were in the barracks. And then we got interrupted.

speaker: interviewee
text: After three days. Oh, this was the first selection, of course. They told us to get out naked. And-- I was never heavy, and definitely not at that time. And I was walking, and I was just thinking how to make myself look more healthy and heavy. But he didn't notice me, luckily. [LAUGHS] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Three weeks passed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he take anybody else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. A woman, I remember, had under the arm-- I-- I saw something. I don't know what happened to her, but right around everybody was suppose to raise their arms up to-- you know, they should see that there is nothing under my arm.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was the beautiful time in Auschwitz. It was fall, and it-- it was cold already. Good and cold. And they undressed us. And-- and-- naked completely. And there was a big pile of sweaters. And I wanted to take a sweater because I had very thin little dress or a blouse and skirt. I don't-- don't remember. I only wanted that sweater.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was pulling it and pulling it. I was sure I have it, then all of a sudden somebody hit me in my head. But was a fist and my head shook. And it wasn't a German. It was another Jewish woman that was in charge of the sweaters.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after she hit me, she gave me the sweater, but my luck didn't work still. It had short sleeves. But it was better than nothing. Why did she hit you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because she felt that she's important. She was supposed to watch it. Nobody should take it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where did the sweaters go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who knows where? Where the children's shoes, and-- and the gold teeth. To the same place.

speaker: interviewer
text: What barracks were you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon.

speaker: interviewer
text: What barracks were you in in Auschwitz? What was it called?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh oh oh oh. Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people in the barrack with you?

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you all-- were you all Schindler people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And they used to give us coffee. In the morning, we had to bring ourselves a huge bucket of coffee. But know what they did with that coffee? Washed the tiles on the floor. Nobody ever got a drop of that coffee in the barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do for drink in fall?

speaker: interviewee
text: We melt snow if there was some.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in charge of you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been one woman, but I don't even remember. And there was that-- that German that came on the train with us that thought she's going also on vacation. She used to come and count us in the middle of the night and the afternoon. Always counting, counting. But I didn't have to go because I was making gloves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she decent to you? That woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: She never hit anybody, but-- but she was very unpleasant. Very, very unpleasant. Just to look at her was enough to scare you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then after you got the orders, that-- that-- it was your whole barracks that was gonna be transported to Brinnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because these were the-- the Schindler people. So we went as-- as a group, as a matter-- two small children went also. Two little girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened? So after they got the orders that you had to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: So they loaded us on the cattle train. And we-- it was so cold. Unbelievably cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do to keep warm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. We were just hugging each other. And the bucket ever so often had to be emptied. And we were counting the days, and counting the days, and then finally we came out. We looked awful because the men were there already prior to-- to women. And when we came, they were waiting because they said that the women are supposed to come. And they started screaming, some women are coming! But they are not our women, because we look awful. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was just three weeks that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it-- and it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course, changed you completely.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- and what was it like when you saw all the men again?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was heavenly. Just that we didn't have where to sleep. They put straw on the concrete floor. Because this use to be a factory where-- where we brought us to Germany. To-- so that-- And we slept on that straw, but it was fantastic.

speaker: interviewer
text: To be out of that camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then--

speaker: interviewee
text: To have the soup, warm soup with horse meat, I couldn't eat it. So I used to get for the portion a piece of bread. I changed it with somebody who liked the horse meat better than I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so what-- what date was this that you got to Brinnlitz? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Must have been in November. End of November.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do when you got to Brinnlitz? You--

speaker: interviewee
text: I did work on the factory. And again, nothing came out. The ammunition never did. And this was about it. The most exciting time was when we got the piece of bread. And God forbid if somebody dropped that bread on the floor. Forget it. You couldn't find it any more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that Schindler's behavior had changed at all, or his attitude had changed? Or was just the same?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see any change in his attitude. Absolutely not.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was still just trying to help you just the same?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was his birthday. Schindler's birthday when we were still in Brinnlitz. And the war was still in existence. But it was toward the end, and we wanted to observe his birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: So somebody gathered white bread and margarine, and made a cake out of it. And we sang to him happy birthday in German and Polish. And he said, maybe this was his handiwork. I knew that it was, that he said, just think for a moment how lucky you are to be in this part of the world right now where a million of German soldiers are being killed [? still. ?] This was the only remark he made as far as the situation of what-- and then few days later, he left the camp. Did you have any warning that the war was coming to an end?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he tell you, or you just heard?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we had little idea and at night somebody was listening to the radio in-- in the morning when you-- every step that the American were taking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the German guards-- did they-- did their attitudes change towards you?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the end? Of course, they were afraid of their own skin. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it change? Can you describe?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were more talkative, and-- and, you know, they were afraid. One of-- of the guards-- but he was a regular killer-- was hanged by the Jewish people the day the war ended.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was hanged in-- in Brinnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he-- I'm telling you, he was a criminal that was in-- in jail for life, but they didn't keep him. They threw him out into concentration camp as Jews. And he was a regular killer. And this was the moment we knew that the war is over. And the radio was blasting. We had some soup with horse meat. And the group of-- of Jewish underground boys grab him and hang him. I didn't look.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was an underground in Brinnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was, and it wasn't, really. No. No. It wasn't organized. It couldn't have been organized at all. Some people claimed that they were part of it, but no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened to the Germans? They hanged this man, and when they--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans ran away and Schindler left also. There was an entourage of people that wanted to go with him and protect him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he ask for help?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know that he asked. I think people volunteered. They wanted to. They felt that they should. But I want you to know another thing. The camp was supposed to be finished before the war ended. And there was a German that was in charge of it. He was suppose to see to it that it's done.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she got him drunk and pushed him off the staircase. Metal spiral staircase. And then they never did anything to us after that. He died?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was either him or us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Schindler took care of it for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what-- after-- after Schindler left and the Germans had left, what happened to the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- We went out, and we tried to get food, some potatoes. And Russians were all ready. Russians came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did somebody-- did somebody come and liberate the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Russian soldiers, sure. We were liberated by Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they bring in supplies for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't have supplies for themselves. They used to say, we brought you kartoshka. Kartoshka means potatoes, but [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we didn't want their potatoes. We didn't want a damn-- the first train that came, many people got on the train or went to Prague in Czechoslovakia. And from there, further.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when you knew that-- that the Germans had gone? And that you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't describe to you the feeling. Absolutely not.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what did the Russians feel when they came into the camp? What did they-- did they say what--

speaker: interviewee
text: We couldn't understand them, first of all because they spoke only Russian. So even if they wanted to maybe condemn us, because not everybody survive and we did, they couldn't say. But they were keeping us and everybody had to clap hands. You know, this was all propaganda again. But this time, Russian. We'd like them, we didn't want them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after the Germans left, what did you decide to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll try to go out west. So we went to Prague. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother and the people that were with us. That's what we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back--

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to the consulate.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to Cracow?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never went back to Cracow. And I don't want to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to a DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I went to Paris. And in Paris, we got a-- a house that used to be, before the war, for students. So they turned this whole house into a dormitory for people from concentration camp. From there, we finally went to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you in Paris for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four years I was waiting for the visa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Paris, in France.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't even know better. I really cannot tell you exactly what year. Everything seems to be like a different dream, different life than--

speaker: interviewer
text: What day did you come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came in May, but I don't remember what date. Maybe May 22, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: 9, yes. Because 1950, my son was born .

speaker: interviewer
text: So who did you come with? You and who else?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband at that time. Yeah. And my brother was already in United States. And his future wife and her parents. So when we came, we already had somebody here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody help you, or any-- like the [? highest ?] or anybody help you to get--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I'll tell you why. Because somebody from the [? highest ?] came on the ship and ask us whether we have any money. And since we had $20, we said yes, we have $20. So we didn't qualify for any support whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not from the Joint Distribution--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not from nobody. Other people went to a free hotel, but not us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do when you came to this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very shortly, I got a job. I worked at Helena Rubinstein Cosmetic Factory. Yes, I was pushing the-- [LAUGHS] and I was pregnant pretty soon. But-- I did do-- I did work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and how many children do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have two. I have a son, big son, and a big daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever discuss the Holocaust with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: For the longest time I never mentioned it to them. But then they showed something for the first time on television. And I made them watched with me the first evening. And I said, if you want to watch the following, it's up to you. Whether they watched or not, I don't know. But they were aware of it. And then came the tape. Henry and my [? cell ?] [? mates, ?] this was on television, all of it. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we-- we finish up, is there any-- is there anything that I haven't asked you about that you'd like to talk about? Something, you know, any incident?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we covered pretty-- pretty well. Yes, pretty much.

speaker: interviewer
text: There's nothing that comes to mind that you want to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any message that you want to give for future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: They should be aware that these things happened And it's not just that-- that the happens one in a million years, because the feelings against Jewish people existed this long, probably as-- as the people are here. So they should be aware of it. And they first of all, remember that we have a state of Israel now. We have a place to support Israel. That's about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you go when they-- when they did this Schindler movie, did you go--

speaker: interviewee
text: To see it? No, I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, did you-- did you go-- were you part of the survivors who went to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Because Mr. Paige at the time, was organizing the-- the trip. And whoever gave him money went. And I didn't think that I should give money for it, so I didn't go. But I went later on. I have pictures of Schindler's grave. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you ever meet Schindler's wife after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Once, I saw her, yes. As a matter of fact, it was in a hotel in New York. And we established a fund for Mrs. Schindler. And she used to receive money. I don't know whether it's still existing or not, regularly from us. She was a decent woman. A very unhappy woman. But a decent human being.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she ever help you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not me, personally. But she was helping. I told you that they took in the transport from the hospital that came in the wintertime. She took care of these people.

speaker: interviewer
text: She makes sure that they don't eat too much at-- at the beginning, you know? That they are being bathed and dressed and everything. This was her-- her project. Do you think that the-- the Schindler movie-- did you ever read the book? Schindler-- you say you didn't see the movie.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see the movie. I don't want to see the movie. And I didn't read the book. I have a book that I got from Mr. Kennedy. You know who Kennedy is? OK. It was his dedication on it. And then there was a-- a evening or something in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Miami. And Kennedy was there, so I went to see it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do-- you you think that it differs vastly from what actually happened? Do you think that people have got the wrong story, the wrong impression?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe, because it was distorted. Because the movie wasn't really-- it-- it was a movie to attract people to go. I didn't want to see that movie because to me, it wasn't the-- the real thing. It was made into a money making proposition and that's all. Mr. Paige made a lot of money and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody was the-- this is the-- the right, here. I swore nobody gave him the right. It was supposed to give him money if they using Schindler's in any way. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Not one of my most beloved people, [LAUGHS] Mr. Paige.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're more than welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I just ask you one more question?

speaker: interviewee
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was Mr. Paige? Can you just describe to me who he was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mr. Paige was a boy from Cracow. Not very bright, but very good looking. And he loved his uniform. He was in Polish Army, and he was a professor of gymnastics. Very bright, he was not. But a very, very good opportunist, and he sensed the opportunity and he grabbed it. Of course, there is not one word truth in it. Starting from the fact that he had a store in Los Angeles with luggage, it's true. But the fellow that came to him from the blue sky was sent by somebody from Australia, and this was the group that decided to make money on Schindler.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Paige.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Sure. [LAUGHS] Of course. Very good looking, you know, tall and handsome.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he-- did he go through-- through all your experience with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, he had to go. He was in Brinnlitz the same way we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: And Plaszow. In Plaszow, he as a Jewish policeman already, and he used to beat people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when Henry left him, [LAUGHS] played with soup.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm? So later on he came and asked, can I have the soup? The place where he worked was very hot. So he said, oh, it evaporated. He ate the soup.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Paige ate the soup?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. This was Mr. Paige.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was-- was he popular?

speaker: interviewee
text: People that-- that had the same beliefs and the same behavior that Mr. Paige, yes. It was that gang of Mr. [? Rosner ?] and Mrs. [? Rosner. ?] This was one big family. The [? Rosners ?] also the one that played the violin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who else was with that group of people?

speaker: interviewee
text: The [? Rosners ?] and the Paiges. I don't know who else there was. There was a few of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Goldberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Goldberg was in a different group, not with them. No. No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give us the name of your mother and the-- the date?

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait, wait, wait. [? Hold on. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Sidonia Scheck. Picture taken in Cracow before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a picture of my brother, George Scheck. And he's toasting my son at his bar mitzvah. The picture was taken at the bar mitzvah of my son. How many years ago? My son is 40-odd years old now. He was 13. This is my daughter Audrey and her little daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- where is it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken in their apartment, I think in Riverdale, New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year? Recently?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd say maybe two years ago. This is a picture of my son and his family. His wife and his two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Natasha is the little girl, and Neddy is the boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your son's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My son's name is Michael.

speaker: interviewer
text: Last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Michael Hirschfeld.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: His wife-- [LAUGHS] her name is [? Grey ?] Hirschfeld.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think when they were on vacation in Martha's Vineyard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Recently?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, every summer they go there. So this must be either last summer or two summers ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: The date, 1994?
